title: Chapter 11 — Advanced Excel Spreadsheets: Statistical and Data Analysis
CHAPTER 11 —
ADVANCED EXCEL SPREADSHEETS: STATISTICAL AND DATA ANALYSIS

Chapter 11 — Advanced Excel Spreadsheets — The mean is the sum divided by the count. If ten customers generate revenues of 1,250, 890, 3,420, 450, 5,100, 620, 2,80

beats.text: Start with the simplest case. You have a column of numbers , revenues from fifty customers who received campaign A. You want to understand what those numbers are doing. Summary statistics are the tools for this. The mean is the sum divided by the count. If ten customers generate revenues of 1,250, 890, 3,420, 450, 5,100, 620, 2,800, 1,950, 4,100, and 2,300, the mean is their sum (22,880) divided by ten, which is 2,288. Excel calculates this with =AVERAGE().

segments.label: 01 / 10 · HOOK

beats.text: A pivot table takes raw data with many rows and many columns and reorganizes it into a summary grid. The word "pivot" is apt: you rotate the data , change what is in the rows, what is in the columns , and see it from different angles without touching the raw data itself. Here is why you need one. The campaign data has 2,847 rows. Each row is one customer. The columns are customer ID, campaign (A or B), region (North, South, East, West), and revenue.

title: WHAT REGRESSION
TELLS YOU.

chance of seeing a relationship this strong if there were ac

beats.text: Summary statistics describe one variable at a time. A pivot table shows how one variable groups by another. Regression asks a different question: does knowing X help you predict Y? If advertising spend goes up by ten thousand dollars, how much does revenue go up? Linear regression finds the equation of the form $y = a + bx$ that best fits a scatter of points.

segments.label: 03 / 10 · WHAT REGRESSION TELLS YOU

title: WORKING BACKWARD:
GOAL SEEK
segments.body_html:
Sometimes you know where you want to end up and need to find the input. Revenue must be 300,000 next quarter. Advertisin

Working Backward: Goal Seek — You could solve the equation by hand: $300,000 = 100,000 + 2.5 \times \text{spend}$, so spend = 80,000. Excel's Goal See

beats.text: Sometimes you know where you want to end up and need to find the input. Revenue must be 300,000 next quarter. Advertising spend is the lever. The regression equation gives you the relationship. What spend is required? You could solve the equation by hand: $300,000 = 100,000 + 2.5 \times \text{spend}$, so spend = 80,000. Excel's Goal Seek does this automatically, and it is useful when the equation is embedded in a complex model where solving by hand is not straightforward.

segments.label: 04 / 10 · WORKING BACKWARD: GOAL SEEK

segments.section: THE THREE TOOLS AS A WORKFLOW
title: THE THREE TOOLS AS
A WORKFLOW.
segments.body_html: First question: what is the data doing?→Second question: how does the data vary across groups?→Third question: does one variable predict another?

beats.text: Summary statistics, pivot tables, and regression are not three separate things to memorize. They are three questions asked in sequence. First question: what is the data doing? Summary statistics answer this. Mean, median, and standard deviation describe the central tendency and spread of each variable individually. Before you do anything else, understand what you have. If the mean and median are far apart, the distribution is skewed and the mean will mislead you.

segments.label: 05 / 10 · THE THREE TOOLS AS A WORKFLOW

beats.text: 11.1 (Mean vs. median , predict before you calculate.) You have seven customer revenues: 500, 800, 1,200, 900, 600, 700, 15,000. Without calculating, predict: will the mean be higher than, lower than, or close to the median? Then calculate both. Was your prediction correct? Explain in one sentence why the mean and median diverged or did not.

beats.text: The ideas in this chapter didn't appear from nowhere. C. R. Rao was working on the Cramér, Rao bound, the Rao, Blackwell theorem, and decades of foundational statistical theory , the math behind the confidence intervals every regression and A/B test silently relies on decades before most people had heard of statistical analysis in spreadsheets. Here's a prompt to find out more , and then make it better.
